items.front: To what operations may MARSA be applied?
items.back: Only to military operations specified in a letter of agreement or other appropriate FAA or military document.

items.front: Who has the command prerogative for MARSA?
items.back: The military command.

items.front: May ATC invoke or deny MARSA?
items.back: No. ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA.

items.front: What is ATC's sole responsibility concerning MARSA operations?
items.back: To provide separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA operations and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

items.front: Who must ensure pilots requesting special use airspace or ATCAAs have coordinated, obtained approval, and know the MARSA procedures?
items.back: DoD.

items.front: Who assigns responsibility and provides separation among participating MARSA aircraft?
items.back: The documented MARSA terms of use authorized by the military command, not ATC.

items.front: Why are military procedures prescribed as additions, modifications, or exceptions to FAA procedure?
items.back: When a common procedure has not been attained or to fulfill a specific requirement.

items.front: Which facilities apply the procedures of the military service that operates them?
items.back: ATC facilities operated by that military service.

items.front: When does a facility's parent organization not control which military procedures are applied?
items.back: When the facility exclusively supports a designated military airport; the designation determines the procedures.

items.front: How may other ATC facilities apply military procedures?
items.back: When specified in a letter of agreement.

items.front: What does the FAA/Naval Air Station example say about an FAA facility serving both a naval station and a civil airport?
items.back: Basic FAA procedures are applied at both locations by that FAA facility.

items.front: How are formation flights controlled?
items.back: As a single aircraft.

items.front: Who has separation responsibility between aircraft within the formation?
items.back: The flight leader and the pilots of the other aircraft in the formation.

items.front: What three conditions must exist for a two-aircraft formation join-up to be supported?
items.back: A participating pilot requests it, all participating pilots concur, and either pilot reports the other in sight.

items.front: From whom is confirmation obtained when multiple single aircraft request to join up or aircraft join an established formation?
items.back: From the lead aircraft.

items.front: For a standard formation after join-up, who squawks the assigned beacon code?
items.back: Only the lead aircraft; the other aircraft squawk standby.

items.front: For a nonstandard formation, what is the normal beacon-code expectation?
items.back: Each aircraft should squawk an ATC-assigned beacon code, although standby may be used if operationally advantageous.

items.front: Who may receive break-up instructions directly when a formation requests break-up?
items.back: The lead or the requesting aircraft in the formation, depending on how the control instructions or clearances are issued.

items.front: At or above FL290, what determines whether RVSM or non-RVSM separation standards are used for a formation flight?
items.back: Whether all aircraft in the formation are RVSM approved.

items.front: What must be ensured before an aircraft enters another controller's area of jurisdiction?
items.back: That the necessary coordination has been accomplished.

items.front: Before directly issuing a heading, route, speed, or altitude change that affects another area, what must be done?
items.back: Ensure coordination has been completed with all affected controllers unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise.

items.front: If the instruction will be relayed through FSS, radio, or another pilot, who still owns the coordination responsibility?
items.back: The controller issuing the control instruction.

items.front: Does ensuring required coordination always mean the issuing controller personally performs the coordination action?
items.back: No.

items.front: What is identified as good operating practice in unusual circumstances such as recent airspace or route changes?
items.back: Confirming that required coordination has been or will be effected.

items.front: What is one listed way control may be transferred?
items.back: At a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude.

items.front: What additional authorization can permit transfer after a radar handoff and frequency change are complete?
items.back: A facility directive or letter of agreement specifying the type and extent of transferred control.

items.front: What must be eliminated before you transfer control of an aircraft?
items.back: Any potential conflict with other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility.

items.front: When may you assume control of an aircraft before it is in your area of jurisdiction?
items.back: Only when specifically coordinated or as specified by a letter of agreement or facility directive.

items.front: Why does 2-1-15 tie conflict elimination to transfer of control?
items.back: Because completing the mechanics of a handoff does not relieve the transferring controller of unresolved separation responsibility.

items.front: Before issuing a clearance that requires flight within a surface area controlled by a nonapproach control tower, what is normally required?
items.back: Coordinate on an individual aircraft basis with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless an LOA says otherwise.

items.front: When radar traffic advisory service will enter another facility's airspace, what must be coordinated?
items.back: Transit authorization with the appropriate control tower.

items.front: While in contact with a radar facility, is the pilot expected to obtain authorization through each area personally?
items.back: No.

items.front: When are communications transferred to the appropriate facility if required for surface-area operations?
items.back: Prior to operation within the surface area for which the tower has responsibility.

items.front: Why does 2-1-16 place the transit-authorization burden on the radar facility instead of the pilot?
items.back: Because the pilot is not expected to self-coordinate through each area while being worked by a radar facility.
